system.description:
Elves walked out of the Veil's first awakening already poised, as if they had been waiting somewhere offstage for the world to catch up. Tall, long-lived, and unsettlingly precise, they dominate the upper floors of the megacorp arcologies and the back rooms of the sprawl's most exclusive clubs in roughly equal measure. Their senses are tuned a half-step sharper than human baseline — they read micro-expressions like billboards and hear the hum of a hidden camera through three layers of drywall. The downside of that perception is that elves rarely get to be surprised by anything, including their own mistakes.

Centuries of life encourage long games. An elf doesn't burn a bridge; she catalogs it, prices it, and sells it back to the people who built it. In the shadow scene they are over-represented as faces, infiltrators, and riggers — anywhere the job rewards patience and a soft voice. The streets call them stuck-up. The streets are usually right. But when an elf finally moves, she moves like she's been rehearsing for forty years, because she has been.

wizard.tagline: Grace and perception honed over centuries
